All requested collections are already '\n            'installed. If you want to reinstall them, '\n            'consider using `--force`.'\n        )\n        return\n\n    # FIXME: This probably needs to be improved to\n    # FIXME: properly match differing src/type.\n    existing_non_requested_collections = {\n        coll for coll in existing_collections\n        if coll.fqcn not in requested_requirements_names\n    }\n\n    preferred_requirements = (\n        [] if force_deps\n        else existing_non_requested_collections if force\n        else existing_collections\n    )\n    preferred_collections = {\n        # NOTE: No need to include signatures if the collection is already installed\n        Candidate(coll.fqcn, coll.ver, coll.src, coll.type, None)\n        for coll in preferred_requirements\n    }\n    with _display_progress(\"Process install dependency map\"):\n        dependency_map = _resolve_depenency_map(\n            collections,\n            galaxy_apis=apis,\n            preferred_candidates=preferred_collections,\n            concrete_artifacts_manager=artifacts_manager,\n            no_deps=no_deps,\n            allow_pre_release=allow_pre_release,\n            upgrade=upgrade,\n            include_signatures=not disable_gpg_verify,\n            offline=offline,\n        )\n\n    keyring_exists = artifacts_manager.keyring is not None\n    with _display_progress(\"Starting collection install process\"):\n        for fqcn, concrete_coll_pin in dependency_map.items():\n            if concrete_coll_pin.is_virtual:\n                display.vvvv(\n                    \"Encountered {coll!s}, skipping.\".\n                    format(coll=to_text(concrete_coll_pin)),\n                )\n                continue\n\n            if concrete_coll_pin in preferred_collections:\n                display.display(\n                    \"'{coll!s}' is already installed, skipping.\".\n                    format(coll=to_text(concrete_coll_pin)),\n                )\n                continue\n\n            if not disable_gpg_verify and concrete_coll_pin.signatures and not keyring_exists:\n                #           )\n                else:\n                    raise\n\n\n# NOTE: imported in ansible.cli.galaxy\ndef validate_collection_name(name):  # type: (str) -> str\n    \"\"\"Validates the collection    return collection_path\n\n\ndef verify_collections(\n        collections,  # type: t.Iterable[Requirement]\n        search_paths,  # type: t.Iterable[str]\n        apis,  # type: The exception is re-raised so we can sure the thread is finished and not using the display anymore\n        raise\n    finally:\n        display = old_display\n\n\ndef _verify_file_hash(b_path, filename, expected_hash, error_queue):\n    b_file_path = to_bytes(os.path.join(to_text(b_path), filename), errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n\n    if not os.path.isfile(b_file_path):\n        actual_hash = None\n    else:\n        with open(b_file_path, mode='rb') as file_object:\n            actual_hash = _consume_file(file_object)\n\n    if expected_hash != actual_hash:\n        error_queue.append(ModifiedContent(filename=filename, expected=expected_hash, installed=actual_hash))\n\n\ndef _make_manifest():\n    return {\n        'files': [\n            {\n                'name': '.',\n                'ftype': 'dir',\n                'chksum_type': None,\n                'chksum_sha256': None,\n                'format': MANIFEST_FORMAT,\n            },\n        ],\n        'format': MANIFEST_FORMAT,\n    }\n\n\ndef _make_entry(name, ftype, chksum_type='sha256', chksum=None):\n    return {\n        'name': name,\n        'ftype': ftype,\n        'chksum_type': chksum_type if chksum else None,\n        f'chksum_{chksum_type}': chksum,\n        'format': MANIFEST_FORMAT\n    }\n\n\ndef _build_files_manifest(b_collection_path, namespace, name, ignore_patterns,\n                          manifest_control, license_file):\n    # **.yaml **.json **.j2 **.license',\n            'recursive-include playbooks **.yml **.yaml **.json **.license',\n            'recursive-include changelogs **.yml **.yaml **.license',\n for d, l in get_all_plugin_loaders())\n        for plugin in sorted(plugins):\n            if plugin in ('modules', 'module_utils'):\n                continue\n            elif plugin in = to_text(b_rel_path, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n\n            if os.path.isdir(b_abs_path):\n                if any(b_item == b_path for b_path in b_ignore_dirs) or \\\n                        any(fnmatch.fnmatch(b_rel_path, b_pattern) for b_pattern in b_ignore_patterns):\n                    display.vvv(\"Skipping '%s' for collection build\" % to_text(b_abs_path))\n                    continue\n\n                if os.path.islink(b_abs_path):\n                    b_link_target = os.path.realpath(b_abs_path)\n\n                    if not _is_child_path(b_link_target, b_top_level_dir):\n                        display.warning(\"Skipping '%s' as it is a symbolic link to a directory outside the collection\"\n                                        % to_text(b_abs_path))\n                        continue\n\n                manifest['files'].append(_make_entry(rel_path, 'dir'))\n\n                if not os.path.islink(b_abs_path):\n                    _walk(b_abs_path, b_top_level_dir)\n            else:\n                if any(fnmatch.fnmatch(b_rel_path, b_pattern) for b_pattern in b_ignore_patterns):\n                    display.vvv(\"Skipping '%s' for collection build\" % to_text(b_abs_path))\n                    continue\n\n                # Handling of file symlinks occur in _build_collection_tar, the manifest for a symlink is the same for\n                # a normal file.\n                manifest['files'].append(\n                    _make_entry(\n                        rel_path,\n                        'file',\n                        chksum_type='sha256',\n                        chksum=secure_hash(b_abs_path, hash_func=sha256)\n                    )\n                )\n\n    _walk(b_collection_path, b_collection_path)\n\n    return manifest\n\n\n# FIXME: accept a dict produced from `galaxy.yml` instead of separate args\ndef _build_manifest(namespace, name, version, authors, readme, tags, description, license_file,\n                    dependencies, repository, documentation, homepage, issues, **kwargs):\n    manifest = {\n        'collection_info': {\n            'namespace': namespace,\n            'name': name,\n            'version': version,\n            'authors': authors,\n            'readme': readme,\n            'tags': tags,\n            'description': description,\n            'license': kwargs['license'],\n            'license_file': license_file or None,  # Handle galaxy.yml having an empty string (None)\n            'dependencies': dependencies,\n            'repository': repository,\n            'documentation': documentation,\n            'homepage': homepage,\n            'issues': issues,\n        },\n        'file_manifest_file': {\n            'name': 'FILES.json',\n            'ftype': 'file',\n            'chksum_type': 'sha256',\n            'chksum_sha256': None,  # Filled out in _build_collection_tar\n            'format': MANIFEST_FORMAT\n        },\n        'format': MANIFEST_FORMAT,\n    }\n\n    return manifest\n\n\ndef _build_collection_tar(\n        b_collection_path,  # type: bytes\n        b_tar_path,  # type: bytes\n        collection_manifest,  # type: CollectionManifestType\n        file_manifest,  # type: FilesManifestType\n):  # type: (...) -> str\n    \"\"\"Build a tar.gz collection artifact from the manifest secure_hash_s(files_manifest_json, hash_func=sha256)\n    collection_manifest_json = to_bytes(json.dumps(collection_manifest, indent=True), errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n\n    with _tempdir() as b_temp_path:\n        b_tar_filepath = os.path.join(b_temp_path, os.path.basename(b_tar_path))\n\n        with tarfile.open(b_tar_filepath, mode='w:gz') as tar_file:\n            # Add the MANIFEST.json and FILES.json file to the archive\n            for name, b in [(MANIFEST_FILENAME, collection_manifest_json), ('FILES.json', files_manifest_json)]:\n                b_io = BytesIO(b)\n                tar_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name)\n                tar_info.size = len(b)\n                tar_info.mtime = int(time.time())\n                tar_info.mode = S_IRWU_RG_RO\n                tar_file.addfile(tarinfo=tar_info, fileobj=b_io)\n\n            for file_info in file_manifest['files']:  # type: ignore[union-attr]\n                if file_info['name'] == '.':\n                    continue\n\n                # arcname expects a native string, cannot be bytes\n                filename = to_native(file_info['name'], errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n                b_src_path = os.path.join(b_collection_path, to_bytes(filename, errors='surrogate_or_strict'))\n\n                def reset_stat(tarinfo):\n                    if tarinfo.type != tarfile.SYMTYPE:\n                        existing_is_exec = tarinfo.mode & stat.S_IXUSR\n                        tarinfo.mode = S_IRWXU_RXG_RXO if existing_is_exec or tarinfo.isdir() else S_IRWU_RG_RO\n                    tarinfo.uid = tarinfo.gid = 0\n                    tarinfo.uname = tarinfo.gname = ''\n\n                    return tarinfo\n\n                if os.path.islink(b_src_path):\n                    b_link_target = os.path.realpath(b_src_path)\n                    if not os.path.exists(b_link_target):\n                        raise AnsibleError(f\"Failed to find the target path '{to_native(b_link_target)}' for the symlink '{to_native(b_src_path)}'.\")\n                    if _is_child_path(b_link_target, b_collection_path):\n                        b_rel_path = os.path.relpath(b_link_target, to_native(b_rel_path, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n                        tar_info = reset_stat(tar_info)\n                        tar_file.addfile(tarinfo=tar_info)\n\n                        continue\n\n                # Dealing with a normal file, just add it by name.\n                tar_file.add(\n                    to_native(os.path.realpath(b_src_path)),\n                    arcname=filename,\n                    recursive=False,\n                    filter=reset_stat,\n                )\n\n        shutil.copy(to_native(b_tar_filepath), to_native(b_tar_path))\n        collection_name = \"%s.%s\" % (collection_manifest['collection_info']['namespace'],\n                                     collection_manifest['collection_info']['name'])\n        tar_path = to_text(b_tar_path)\n        display.display(u'Created collection for %s at %s' % (collection_name, tar_path))\n        return tar_path\n\n\ndef _build_collection_dir(b_collection_path, b_collection_output, collection_manifest, file_manifest):\n    \"\"\"Build a collection directory from the manifest data.\n\n    This should follow the same pattern as _build_collection_tar.\n    \"\"\"\n    os.makedirs(b_collection_output, mode=S_IRWXU_RXG_RXO)\n\n    files_manifest_json = to_bytes(json.dumps(file_manifest, indent=True), errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n    collection_manifest['file_manifest_file']['chksum_sha256'] = secure_hash_s(files_manifest_json, hash_func=sha256)\n    collection_manifest_json = to_bytes(json.dumps(collection_manifest, indent=True), errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n\n    # Write contents to the files\n    for name, b in [(MANIFEST_FILENAME, collection_manifest_json), ('FILES.json', files_manifest_json)]:\n        b_path = os.path.join(b_collection_output, to_bytes(name, errors='surrogate_or_strict'))\n        with open(b_path, 'wb') as file_obj, BytesIO(b) as b_io:\n            shutil.copyfileobj(b_io, file_obj)\n\n        os.chmod(b_path, S_IRWU_RG_RO)\n\n    base_directories = []\n    for file_info in sorted(file_manifest['files'], key=lambda x: x['name']):\n        if file_info['name'] == '.':\n            continue\n\n        src_file = os.path.join(b_collection_path, to_bytes(file_info['name'], errors='surrogate_or_strict'))\n        dest_file = os.path.join(b_collection_output, to_bytes(file_info['name'], errors='surrogate_or_strict'))\n\n        existing_is_exec = os.stat(src_file, follow_symlinks=False).st_mode & stat.S_IXUSR\n        mode = S_IRWXU_RXG_RXO if existing_is_exec else S_IRWU_RG_RO\n\n        # ensure symlinks to dirs are not translated to empty dirs\n        if os.path.isdir(src_file) and not os.path.islink(src_file):\n            mode =    return collection_output\n\n\ndef _normalize_collection_path(path):\n    str_path = path.as_posix() if isinstance(path, pathlib.Path) else path\n    return pathlib.Path(\n        # This is annoying, but GalaxyCLI._resolve_path did it\n        os.path.expandvars(str_path)\n    ).expanduser().absolute()\n\n\ndef find_existing_collections(path_filter, artifacts_manager, namespace_filter=None, collection_filter=None, dedupe=True):\n    \"\"\"Locate all collections under a given path.\n\n    :param path: Collection dirs layout search path.\n    :param artifacts_manager: Artifacts manager.\n    \"\"\"\n    if files is None:\n        raise AnsibleError('importlib_resources is not installed and is required')\n\n    if path_filter and not is_sequence(path_filter):\n        path_filter = [path_filter]\n    if namespace_filter and not is_sequence(namespace_filter):\n        namespace_filter = [namespace_filter]\n    if collection_filter and not is_sequence(collection_filter):\n        collection_filter = [collection_filter]\n\n    paths = set()\n    for path in files('ansible_collections').glob('*/*/'):\n        path = _normalize_collection_path(path)\n        if not path.is_dir():\n            continue\n        if path_filter:\n            for pf in path_filter:\n                try:\n                    path.relative_to(_normalize_collection_path(pf))\n                except ValueError:\n                    continue\n                break\n            else:\n                continue\n        paths.add(path)\n\n    seen = set()\n    for path in paths:\n        namespace = path.parent.name\n        name = path.name\n        if namespace_filter and namespace not in namespace_filter:\n            continue\n        if collection_filter and name not in collection_filter:\n            continue\n\n        if dedupe:\n            try:\n                collection_path = files(f'ansible_collections.{namespace}.{name}')\n            except ImportError:\n                continue\n            if collection_path in seen:\n                continue\n            seen.add(collection_path)\n        else:\n            collection_path = path\n\n        b_collection_path = to_bytes(collection_path.as_posix())\n\n        try:\n            req = Candidate.from_dir_path_as_unknown(b_collection_path, artifacts_manager)\n        except ValueError as val_err:\n            display.warning(f'{val_err}')\n            continue\n\n        display.vvv(\n            u\"Found installed collection {coll!s} at '{path!s}'\".\n            format(coll=to_text(req), path=to_text(req.src))\n        )\n        yield req\n\n\ndef install(collection, path, artifacts_manager):  # FIXME: mv to dataclasses?\n    # type: (Candidate, str, ConcreteArtifactsManager) -> None\n    \"\"\"Install a collection under a given path.\n\n    :param collection: Collection to be installed.\n    :param path: Collection dirs layout path.\n    :param artifacts_manager: Artifacts manager.\n    \"\"\"\n    b_artifact_path = artifacts_manager.get_artifact_path_from_unknown(collection)\n\n    collection_path = os.path.join(path, collection.namespace, collection.name)\n    b_collection_path = to_bytes(collection_path, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n    display.display(\n        u\"Installing '{coll!s}' to '{path!s}'\".\n        format(coll=to_text(collection), path=collection_path),\n    )\n\n    if os.path.exists(b_collection_path):\n        shutil.rmtree(b_collection_path)\n\n    if collection.is_dir:\n        install_src(collection, b_artifact_path, b_collection_path, artifacts_manager)\n    else:\n        install_artifact(\n            b_artifact_path,\n            b_collection_path,\n            artifacts_manager._b_working_directory,\n            collection.signatures,\n            artifacts_manager.keyring,\n            artifacts_manager.required_successful_signature_count,\n            artifacts_manager.ignore_signature_errors,\n        )\n        remove_source_metadata(collection, b_collection_path)\n        if (collection.is_online_index_pointer and isinstance(collection.src, GalaxyAPI)):\n            write_source_metadata(\n                collection,\n                b_collection_path,\n                artifacts_manager\n            )\n\n    display.display(\n        '{coll!s} was installed successfully'.\n        format(coll=to_text(collection)),\n    )\n\n\ndef write_source_metadata(collection, b_collection_path, artifacts_manager):\n    # type: (Candidate, bytes, ConcreteArtifactsManager) -> None\n    source_data = artifacts_manager.get_galaxy_artifact_source_info(collection)\n\n    b_yaml_source_data = to_bytes(yaml_dump(source_data), errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n    b_info_dest = collection.construct_galaxy_info_path(b_collection_path)\n    b_info_dir = os.path.split(b_info_dest)[0]\n\n    if os.path.exists(b_info_dir):\n        shutil.rmtree(b_info_dir)\n\n    try:\n        os.mkdir(b_info_dir, mode=S_IRWXU_RXG_RXO)\n        with open(b_info_dest, mode='w+b') as fd:\n            fd.write(b_yaml_source_data)\n        os.chmod(b_info_dest, S_IRWU_RG_RO)\n    except Exception:\n        # Ensure we don't leave the dir behind in case of a failure.\n        if os.path.isdir(b_info_dir):\n            shutil.rmtree(b_info_dir)\n        raise\n\n\ndef remove_source_metadata(collection, b_collection_path):\n    pattern = f\"{collection.namespace}.{collection.name}-*.info\"\n    info_path = os.path.join(\n        b_collection_path,\n        b'../../',\n        to_bytes(pattern, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n    )\n    if (outdated_info := glob.glob(info_path)):\n        display.vvvv(f\"Removing {pattern} metadata from previous installations\")\n    for info_dir in outdated_info:\n        try:\n            shutil.rmtree(info_dir)\n        except Exception:\n            pass\n\n\ndef verify_artifact_manifest(manifest_file, signatures, keyring, required_signature_count, ignore_signature_errors):\n    # type: (str, list[str], str, str, list[str]) -> None\n    failed_verify = False\n    coll_path_parts = to_text(manifest_file, errors='surrogate_or_strict').split(os.path.sep)\n    collection_name = '%s.%s' % (coll_path_parts[-3], coll_path_parts[-2])  # get 'ns' and 'coll' from /path/to/ns/coll/MANIFEST.json\n    if not verify_file_signatures(collection_name, manifest_file, signatures, keyring, required_signature_count, ignore_signature_errors):\n        raise AnsibleError(f\"Not installing {collection_name} because GnuPG signature verification failed.\")\n    display.vvvv(f\"GnuPG signature verification succeeded for {collection_name}\")\n\n\ndef install_artifact(b_coll_targz_path, b_collection_path, b_temp_path, signatures, keyring, required_signature_count, ignore_signature_errors):\n    \"\"\"Install a collection from tarball under a given path.\n\n    :param b_coll_targz_path: Collection tarball to be installed.\n    :param b_collection_path: Collection dirs layout path.\n    :param b_temp_path: Temporary dir path.\n    :param signatures: frozenset of signatures to verify the MANIFEST.json\n    :param keyring: The keyring used during GPG verification\n    :param required_signature_count: The number of signatures that must successfully verify the collection\n    :param ignore_signature_errors: GPG errors to ignore during signature verification\n    \"\"\"\n    try:\n        with tarfile.open(b_coll_targz_path, mode='r') as collection_tar:\n            # Remove this once py3.11 is our controller minimum\n            # Workaround for https://bugs.python.org/issue47231\n            # See _extract_tar_dir\n            collection_tar._ansible_normalized_cache = {\n                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in collection_tar.getmembers()\n            }  # deprecated: description='TarFile member index' core_version='2.18' python_version='3.11'\n\n            # Verify the signature on the MANIFEST.json before extracting anything else\n            _extract_tar_file(collection_tar, MANIFEST_FILENAME, b_collection_path, b_temp_path)\n\n            if keyring is not None:\n                manifest_file = os.path.join(to_text(b_collection_path, errors='surrogate_or_strict'), MANIFEST_FILENAME)\n                verify_artifact_manifest(manifest_file, signatures, keyring, required_signature_count, ignore_signature_errors)\n\n            files_member_obj = collection_tar.getmember('FILES.json')\n            with _tarfile_extract(collection_tar, files_member_obj) as (dummy, files_obj):\n                files = json.loads(to_text(files_obj.read(), errors='surrogate_or_strict'))\n\n            _extract_tar_file(collection_tar, 'FILES.json', b_collection_path, b_temp_path)\n\n            for file_info in files['files']:\n                file_name = file_info['name']\n                if file_name == '.':\n                    continue\n\n                if file_info['ftype'] == 'file':\n                    _extract_tar_file(collection_tar, file_name, b_collection_path, b_temp_path,\n                                      expected_hash=file_info['chksum_sha256'])\n\n                else:\n                    _extract_tar_dir(collection_tar, file_name, b_collection_path)\n\n    except Exception:\n        # Ensure we don't leave the dir behind in case of a failure.\n        shutil.rmtree(b_collection_path)\n\n        b_namespace_path = os.path.dirname(b_collection_path)\n        if not os.listdir(b_namespace_path):\n            os.rmdir(b_namespace_path)\n\n        raise\n\n\ndef install_src(collection, b_collection_path, b_collection_output_path, artifacts_manager):\n    r\"\"\"Install the collection from source control into given dir.\n\n    Generates the Ansible collection artifact data from a galaxy.yml and\n    installs the artifact to a directory.\n    This should follow the same pattern as build_collection, but instead\n    of creating an artifact, install it.\n\n    :param collection: Collection to be installed.\n    :param b_collection_path: Collection dirs layout path.\n    :param b_collection_output_path: The installation directory for the \\\n                                     collection artifact.\n    :param artifacts_manager: Artifacts manager.\n\n    :raises AnsibleError: If no collection metadata found.\n    \"\"\"\n    collection_meta = artifacts_manager.get_direct_collection_meta(collection)\n\n    if 'build_ignore' not in collection_meta:  # installed collection, not src\n        # FIXME: optimize this? use a different process? copy instead of build?\n        collection_meta['build_ignore'] = []\n        collection_meta['manifest'] = Sentinel\n    collection_manifest = _build_manifest(**collection_meta)\n    file_manifest = _build_files_manifest(\n        b_collection_path,\n        collection_meta['namespace'], collection_meta['name'],\n        collection_meta['build_ignore'],\n        collection_meta['manifest'],\n        collection_meta['license_file'],\n    )\n\n    collection_output_path = _build_collection_dir(\n        b_collection_path, b_collection_output_path,\n        collection_manifest, file_manifest,\n    )\n\n    display.display(\n        'Created collection for {coll!s} at {path!s}'.\n        format(coll=collection, path=collection_output_path)\n    )\n\n\ndef _extract_tar_dir(tar, dirname, b_dest):\n    \"\"\" Extracts a directory from a collection tar. \"\"\"\n    dirname = to_native(dirname, errors='surrogate_or_strict').removesuffix(os.path.sep)\n\n    try:\n        tar_member = tar._ansible_normalized_cache[dirname]\n    except KeyError:\n        raise AnsibleError(\"Unable to extract '%s' from collection\" % dirname)\n\n    b_dir_path = os.path.join(b_dest, to_bytes(dirname, errors='surrogate_or_strict'))\n\n    b_parent_path = os.path.dirname(b_dir_path)\n    try:\n        os.makedirs(b_parent_path, mode=S_IRWXU_RXG_RXO)\n    except OSError as e:\n        if e.errno != errno.EEXIST:\n            raise\n\n    if tar_member.type == tarfile.SYMTYPE:\n        b_link_path = to_bytes(tar_member.linkname, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n        if not _is_child_path(b_link_path, b_dest, link_name=b_dir_path):\n            raise AnsibleError(\"Cannot extract symlink '%s' in collection: path points to location outside of \"\n                               \"collection '%s'\" % (to_native(dirname), b_link_path))\n\n        os.symlink(b_link_path, b_dir_path)\n\n    else:\n        if not os.path.isdir(b_dir_path):\n            os.mkdir(b_dir_path, S_IRWXU_RXG_RXO)\n\n\ndef _extract_tar_file(tar, filename, b_dest, b_temp_path, expected_hash=None):\n    \"\"\" Extracts a file from a collection tar. \"\"\"\n    with _get_tar_file_member(tar, filename) as (tar_member, tar_obj):\n        if tar_member.type == tarfile.SYMTYPE:\n            actual_hash = _consume_file(tar_obj)\n\n        else:\n            with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(dir=b_temp_path, delete=False) as tmpfile_obj:\n                actual_hash = _consume_file(tar_obj, tmpfile_obj)\n\n        if expected_hash and actual_hash != expected_hash:\n            raise AnsibleError(\"Checksum mismatch for '%s' inside collection at '%s'\"\n                               % (to_native(filename, errors='surrogate_or_strict'), to_native(tar.name)))\n\n        b_dest_filepath = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(b_dest, to_bytes(filename, errors='surrogate_or_strict')))\n        b_parent_dir = os.path.dirname(b_dest_filepath)\n        if not _is_child_path(b_parent_dir, b_dest):\n            raise AnsibleError(\"Cannot extract tar entry '%s' as it will be placed outside the collection directory\"\n                               % to_native(filename, errors='surrogate_or_strict'))\n\n        if not os.path.exists(b_parent_dir):\n            # Seems like Galaxy does not validate if all file entries have a corresponding dir ftype entry. This check\n            # makes sure we create the parent directory even if it wasn't set in the metadata.\n            os.makedirs(b_parent_dir, mode=S_IRWXU_RXG_RXO)\n\n        if tar_member.type == tarfile.SYMTYPE:\n            b_link_path = to_bytes(tar_member.linkname, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n            if not _is_child_path(b_link_path, b_dest, link_name=b_dest_filepath):\n                raise AnsibleError(\"Cannot extract symlink '%s' in collection: path points to location outside of \"\n                                   \"collection '%s'\" % (to_native(filename), b_link_path))\n\n            os.symlink(b_link_path, b_dest_filepath)\n\n        else:\n            shutil.move(to_bytes(tmpfile_obj.name, errors='surrogate_or_strict'), b_dest_filepath)\n\n            # Default to rw-r--r-- and only add execute if the tar file has execute.\n            tar_member = tar.getmember(to_native(filename, errors='surrogate_or_strict'))\n            new_mode = S_IRWU_RG_RO\n            if stat.S_IMODE(tar_member.mode) & stat.S_IXUSR:\n                new_mode |= S_IXANY\n\n            os.chmod(b_dest_filepath, new_mode)\n\n\ndef _get_tar_file_member(tar, filename):\n    n_filename = to_native(filename, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n    try:\n        member = tar.getmember(n_filename)\n    except KeyError:\n        raise AnsibleError(\"Collection tar at '%s' does not contain the expected file '%s'.\" % (\n            to_native(tar.name),\n            n_filename))\n\n    return _tarfile_extract(tar, member)\n\n\ndef _get_json_from_tar_file(b_path, filename):\n    file_contents = ''\n\n    with tarfile.open(b_path, mode='r') as collection_tar:\n        with _get_tar_file_member(collection_tar, filename) as (dummy, tar_obj):\n            bufsize = 65536\n            data = tar_obj.read(bufsize)\n            while data:\n                file_contents += to_text(data)\n                data = tar_obj.read(bufsize)\n\n    return json.loads(file_contents)\n\n\ndef _get_tar_file_hash(b_path, filename):\n    with tarfile.open(b_path, mode='r') as collection_tar:\n        with _get_tar_file_member(collection_tar, filename) as (dummy, tar_obj):\n            return _consume_file(tar_obj)\n\n\ndef _get_file_hash(b_path, filename):  # type: (bytes, str) -> str\n    filepath = os.path.join(b_path, to_bytes(filename, errors='surrogate_or_strict'))\n    with open(filepath, 'rb') as fp:\n        return _consume_file(fp)\n\n\ndef _is_child_path(path, parent_path, link_name=None):\n    \"\"\" Checks that path is a path within the parent_path specified. \"\"\"\n    b_path = to_bytes(path, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n\n    if link_name and not os.path.isabs(b_path):\n        # If link_name is specified, path is the source of the link and we need to resolve the absolute path.\n        b_link_dir = os.path.dirname(to_bytes(link_name, errors='surrogate_or_strict'))\n        b_path = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(b_link_dir, b_path))\n\n    b_parent_path = to_bytes(parent_path, errors='surrogate_or_strict')\n    return b_path == b_parent_path or b_path.startswith(b_parent_path + to_bytes(os.path.sep))\n\n\ndef _resolve_depenency_map(\n        requested_requirements,  # type: t.Iterable[Requirement]\n        galaxy_apis,  # type: t.Iterable[GalaxyAPI]\n        concrete_artifacts_manager,  # type: ConcreteArtifactsManager\n        preferred_candidates,  # type: t.Iterable[Candidate] | None\n        no_deps,  # type: bool\n        allow_pre_release,  # type: bool\n        upgrade,  # type: bool\n        include_signatures,  # type: bool\n        offline,  # type: bool\n):  # type: (...) -> dict[str, Candidate]\n    \"\"\"Return the resolved dependency map.\"\"\"\n    if not HAS_RESOLVELIB:\n        raise AnsibleError(\"Failed to import resolvelib, check that a supported version is installed\")\n    if not HAS_PACKAGING:\n        raise AnsibleError(\"Failed to import packaging, check that a supported version is installed\")\n\n    req = None\n\n    try:\n        dist = distribution('ansible-core')\n    except Exception:\n        pass\n    else:\n        req = next((rr for r in (dist.requires or []) if (rr := PkgReq(r)).name == 'resolvelib'), None)\n    finally:\n        if req is None:\n            # TODO: replace the hardcoded versions with a warning if the dist info is missing\n            # display.warning(\"Unable to find 'ansible-core' distribution requirements to verify the resolvelib version is supported.\")\n            if not RESOLVELIB_LOWERBOUND <= RESOLVELIB_VERSION < RESOLVELIB_UPPERBOUND:\n                raise AnsibleError(\n                    f\"ansible-galaxy requires resolvelib<{RESOLVELIB_UPPERBOUND.vstring},>={RESOLVELIB_LOWERBOUND.vstring}\"\n                )\n        elif not req.specifier.contains(RESOLVELIB_VERSION.vstring):\n            raise AnsibleError(f\"ansible-galaxy requires {req.name}{req.specifier}\")\n\n    pre_release_hint = '' if allow_pre_release else (\n        'Hint: Pre-releases hosted on Galaxy or Automation Hub are not '\n        'installed by default unless a specific version is requested. '\n        'To enable pre-releases globally, use --pre.'\n    )\n\n    collection_dep_resolver = build_collection_dependency_resolver(\n        galaxy_apis=galaxy_apis,\n        concrete_artifacts_manager=concrete_artifacts_manager,\n        preferred_candidates=preferred_candidates,\n        with_deps=not no_deps,\n        with_pre_releases=allow_pre_release,\n        upgrade=upgrade,\n        include_signatures=include_signatures,\n        offline=offline,\n    )\n    try:\n        return collection_dep_resolver.resolve(\n            requested_requirements,\n            max_rounds=2000000,  # NOTE: same constant pip uses\n        ).mapping\n    except CollectionDependencyResolutionImpossible as dep_exc:\n        conflict_causes = (\n            '* {req.fqcn!s}:{req.ver!s} ({dep_origin!s})'.format(\n                req=req_inf.requirement,\n                dep_origin='direct request'\n                if req_inf.parent is None\n                else 'dependency of {parent!s}'.\n                format(parent=req_inf.parent),\n            )\n            for req_inf in dep_exc.causes\n        )\n        error_msg_lines = list(chain(\n            (\n                'Failed to resolve the requested '\n                'dependencies map. Could not satisfy the following '\n                'requirements:',\n            ),\n            conflict_causes,\n        ))\n        error_msg_lines.append(pre_release_hint)\n        raise AnsibleError('\\n'.join(error_msg_lines)) from dep_exc\n    except CollectionDependencyInconsistentCandidate as dep_exc:\n        parents = [\n            str(p) for p in dep_exc.criterion.iter_parent()\n            if p is not None\n        ]\n\n        error_msg_lines = [\n            (\n                'Failed to resolve the requested dependencies map. '\n                'Got the candidate {req.fqcn!s}:{req.ver!s} ({dep_origin!s}) '\n                'which didn\\'t satisfy all of the following requirements:'.\n                format(\n                    req=dep_exc.candidate,\n                    dep_origin='direct request'\n                    if not parents else 'dependency of {parent!s}'.\n                    format(parent=', '.join(parents))\n                )\n            )\n        ]\n\n        for req in dep_exc.criterion.iter_requirement():\n            error_msg_lines.append(\n                f'* {req.fqcn!s}:{req.ver!s}'\n            )\n        error_msg_lines.append(pre_release_hint)\n\n        raise AnsibleError('\\n'.join(error_msg_lines)) from dep_exc\n    except ValueError as exc:\n        raise AnsibleError(to_native(exc)) from exc\n", "numLines": 1924, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 1924}}

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
"""
Regression test for Galaxy collection tar directory extraction fragility.

This test demonstrates the issue where the custom normalized cache in
_extract_tar_dir creates fragile lookups when tar archives have directory
entries stored with trailing path separators.

The custom cache strips trailing separators when building the index, which
can cause mismatches when archive member names differ in their trailing
separator usage.
"""

import io
import json
import os
import tarfile
import tempfile
import pytest
from pathlib import Path

# Import the function we're testing
from ansible.galaxy.collection import _extract_tar_dir, install_artifact
from ansible.errors import AnsibleError


class TestTarDirectoryExtractionFragility:
    """Test suite for tar directory extraction issues."""

    @pytest.fixture
    def temp_dir(self):
        """Create a temporary directory for test output."""
        with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir:
            yield tmpdir

    @pytest.fixture
    def temp_tar_path(self, temp_dir):
        """Create a temporary tar file path."""
        return os.path.join(temp_dir, "test_collection.tar.gz")

    def create_tar_with_directory_variations(self, tar_path, include_trailing_sep=False):
        """
        Create a tar archive with directory entries.

        This function creates a tar archive that may have directory entries
        stored with or without trailing separators, which is the core of the
        fragility issue.

        :param tar_path: Path to create the tar file
        :param include_trailing_sep: If True, create directory entries WITH trailing separators
        """
        with tarfile.open(tar_path, mode='w:gz') as tar:
            # Create MANIFEST.json
            manifest = {
                'collection_info': {
                    'namespace': 'test',
                    'name': 'collection',
                    'version': '1.0.0',
                    'authors': ['Test'],
                    'license': ['GPL-3.0-or-later'],
                    'description': 'Test collection',
                    'repository': None,
                    'documentation': None,
                    'homepage': None,
                    'issues': None,
                    'readme': None,
                    'tags': [],
                    'dependencies': {},
                }
            }
            manifest_bytes = json.dumps(manifest).encode('utf-8')
            manifest_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name='MANIFEST.json')
            manifest_info.size = len(manifest_bytes)
            tar.addfile(manifest_info, io.BytesIO(manifest_bytes))

            # Create FILES.json with both file and directory entries
            files_manifest = {
                'files': [
                    {'name': '.', 'ftype': 'dir', 'chksum_type': None, 'chksum_sha256': None, 'format': 1},
                    # Directories - the key test is how these are named
                    {'name': 'plugins', 'ftype': 'dir', 'chksum_type': None, 'chksum_sha256': None, 'format': 1},
                    {'name': 'plugins/modules', 'ftype': 'dir', 'chksum_type': None, 'chksum_sha256': None, 'format': 1},
                    # Files
                    {'name': 'plugins/modules/test.py', 'ftype': 'file', 'chksum_type': 'sha256', 'chksum_sha256': 'abc123', 'format': 1},
                    {'name': 'README.md', 'ftype': 'file', 'chksum_type': 'sha256', 'chksum_sha256': 'def456', 'format': 1},
                ]
            }
            files_bytes = json.dumps(files_manifest).encode('utf-8')
            files_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name='FILES.json')
            files_info.size = len(files_bytes)
            tar.addfile(files_info, io.BytesIO(files_bytes))

            # Add directories to tar with or without trailing separators
            # This is the fragility: different tar creators may add trailing separators
            if include_trailing_sep:
                # Standard tar format: directories have trailing separators
                dir_entries = ['plugins/', 'plugins/modules/']
            else:
                # Non-standard but possible: directories without trailing separators
                dir_entries = ['plugins', 'plugins/modules']

            for dir_name in dir_entries:
                dir_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name=dir_name)
                dir_info.type = tarfile.DIRTYPE
                dir_info.mode = 0o755
                tar.addfile(dir_info)

            # Add a simple file
            file_content = b"print('test')"
            file_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name='plugins/modules/test.py')
            file_info.size = len(file_content)
            tar.addfile(file_info, io.BytesIO(file_content))

            # Add README
            readme_content = b"# Test Collection"
            readme_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name='README.md')
            readme_info.size = len(readme_content)
            tar.addfile(readme_info, io.BytesIO(readme_content))

    def test_tar_extraction_with_trailing_separator_directories(self, temp_tar_path, temp_dir):
        """
        Test extraction when tar has directories with trailing separators.

        This tests the standard tar format where directories are stored with
        trailing separators. The normalized cache should handle this correctly.
        """
        self.create_tar_with_directory_variations(temp_tar_path, include_trailing_sep=True)

        # Open the tar and manually test the cache
        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='r:gz') as tar:
            # Build the normalized cache like the code does
            tar._ansible_normalized_cache = {
                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in tar.getmembers()
            }

            # Try to extract directories as the code does in FILES.json processing
            collection_path = os.path.join(temp_dir, 'extract_test_1')
            os.makedirs(collection_path)

            # Extract 'plugins' directory (no trailing separator in manifest)
            # But tar has it as 'plugins/' (with trailing separator)
            try:
                _extract_tar_dir(tar, 'plugins', collection_path.encode('utf-8'))
                assert os.path.isdir(os.path.join(collection_path, 'plugins'))
            except AnsibleError as e:
                pytest.fail(f"Failed to extract directory with trailing separator: {e}")

    def test_tar_extraction_without_trailing_separator_directories(self, temp_tar_path, temp_dir):
        """
        Test extraction when tar has directories WITHOUT trailing separators.

        This tests a non-standard but possible tar layout. This is where the
        fragility becomes apparent - the custom cache may fail or behave
        unexpectedly with this format.
        """
        self.create_tar_with_directory_variations(temp_tar_path, include_trailing_sep=False)

        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='r:gz') as tar:
            # Check what members are actually in the tar
            members = {m.name for m in tar.getmembers()}
            print(f"Tar members: {members}")

            # Build the normalized cache like the code does
            tar._ansible_normalized_cache = {
                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in tar.getmembers()
            }

            print(f"Normalized cache keys: {tar._ansible_normalized_cache.keys()}")

            # Try to extract directories
            collection_path = os.path.join(temp_dir, 'extract_test_2')
            os.makedirs(collection_path)

            # Extract 'plugins' directory
            # If tar has 'plugins' (no separator), cache should have 'plugins'
            try:
                _extract_tar_dir(tar, 'plugins', collection_path.encode('utf-8'))
                assert os.path.isdir(os.path.join(collection_path, 'plugins'))
            except AnsibleError as e:
                pytest.fail(f"Failed to extract directory without trailing separator: {e}")

    def test_normalized_cache_key_collision(self, temp_tar_path):
        """
        Test that demonstrates the fragility: cache key collision.

        When a tar archive could theoretically have both 'plugins' and 'plugins/'
        as separate entries, the normalized cache would create a collision where
        the last one overwrites the first. This shows why the custom cache approach
        is fragile and why the standard tarfile.getmember() API is better.
        """
        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='w:gz') as tar:
            # Create a directory entry WITHOUT trailing separator
            dir_info1 = tarfile.TarInfo(name='plugins')
            dir_info1.type = tarfile.DIRTYPE
            dir_info1.mode = 0o755
            tar.addfile(dir_info1)

            # Create a directory entry WITH trailing separator
            # In a real scenario, these could be added by different tools
            # or in different orders
            dir_info2 = tarfile.TarInfo(name='plugins/')
            dir_info2.type = tarfile.DIRTYPE
            dir_info2.mode = 0o755
            tar.addfile(dir_info2)

        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='r:gz') as tar:
            members = {m.name for m in tar.getmembers()}

            # Verify both entries exist in the tar
            assert 'plugins' in members, "Tar should have 'plugins' entry"
            assert 'plugins/' in members, "Tar should have 'plugins/' entry"

            # Now build the normalized cache - this is where the fragility occurs
            cache = {
                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in tar.getmembers()
            }

            # The cache will only have ONE entry 'plugins' since both normalized to the same key
            assert len(cache) == 1, "Cache should have only 1 entry after normalization"
            assert 'plugins' in cache, "Cache should have 'plugins' key"

            # This is the fragility: we lose information about which tar member
            # was used because the normalization collapses them
            # The actual tar has 2 members, but the cache only tracks 1
            # If code logic depended on having both entries, it would fail
            print(f"Tar has {len(members)} members but normalized cache has {len(cache)} entries")

    def test_custom_cache_vs_standard_getmember(self, temp_tar_path, temp_dir):
        """
        Test that shows why the custom cache is fragile compared to getmember().

        This test demonstrates that the standard tarfile.getmember() API correctly
        handles directory lookups with or without trailing separators, making the
        custom cache workaround unnecessary on Python 3.11+.
        """
        self.create_tar_with_directory_variations(temp_tar_path, include_trailing_sep=True)

        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='r:gz') as tar:
            # Method 1: Using the fragile custom cache
            custom_cache = {
                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in tar.getmembers()
            }

            # Method 2: Using standard getmember() API
            # Modern Python versions (3.11+) handle both cases correctly

            # Test 1: Look up 'plugins' (no trailing separator in tar but with separator in manifest)
            try:
                # With custom cache
                cached_member = custom_cache.get('plugins')

                # With standard API - on modern Python, this should work
                # even if the tar has 'plugins/' with a trailing separator
                try:
                    standard_member = tar.getmember('plugins')
                    print(f"Standard getmember('plugins') found: {standard_member.name}")
                except KeyError:
                    # On older Python, this might fail, requiring the workaround
                    print("Standard getmember('plugins') failed - this is why the workaround was needed")
                    standard_member = None

                # The fragility is that if getmember() behavior changes between
                # Python versions, the custom cache becomes a band-aid that
                # masks the real issue
                assert cached_member is not None, "Custom cache should find directory"
            except Exception as e:
                pytest.fail(f"Directory lookup failed: {e}")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    pytest.main([__file__, '-v'])

entries stored with trailing path separators.

can cause mismatches when archive member names differ in their trailing
separator usage.

            files_manifest = {

def test_normalized_cache_normalization_behavior(self, temp_tar_path):
        """
        Test that demonstrates the fragility: unnecessary normalization adds complexity.

        This test shows that when tar archives store directories with trailing
        separators (the standard), the custom normalized cache strips them during
        lookup. This creates a point of fragility - if the tar member name format
        changes, the cache might fail to find entries.

        The normalization is unnecessary on Python 3.11+ where tarfile.getmember()
        correctly handles directory lookups.
        """
        self.create_tar_with_directory_variations(temp_tar_path, include_trailing_sep=True)

        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='r:gz') as tar:
            members = {m.name for m in tar.getmembers()}

            # In standard tar format, directories have trailing separators
            # E.g., 'plugins/' and 'plugins/modules/'
            dir_members = [m for m in tar.getmembers() if m.isdir()]
            print(f"Directory members in tar: {[m.name for m in dir_members]}")

            # Build the normalized cache like the code does
            cache = {
                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in tar.getmembers()
            }

            # Demonstrate the fragility: the cache has different keys than the tar members
            # This means lookups depend on the normalization being correct
            for m in dir_members:
                normalized_key = m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep)
                # If code is called with the normalized key, it works
                assert normalized_key in cache, f"Normalized key '{normalized_key}' should be in cache"
                # But if the tar member name format changes (e.g., no trailing sep),
                # the relationship breaks
                print(f"Tar member '{m.name}' → cache key '{normalized_key}'")

            # The fragility: if getmember() behavior changed between Python versions
            # (which it did - that's what bug 47231 was about), this workaround
            # becomes the only thing keeping extraction working. But it's brittle
            # because the normalization assumes all directory members have trailing seps.

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
"""
Regression test for Galaxy collection tar directory extraction fragility.

This test demonstrates the issue where the custom normalized cache in
_extract_tar_dir creates fragile lookups when tar archives have directory
entries stored with trailing path separators.

The custom cache strips trailing separators when building the index, which
can cause mismatches when archive member names differ in their trailing
separator usage.
"""

import io
import json
import os
import tarfile
import tempfile
import pytest
from pathlib import Path

# Import the function we're testing
from ansible.galaxy.collection import _extract_tar_dir, install_artifact
from ansible.errors import AnsibleError


class TestTarDirectoryExtractionFragility:
    """Test suite for tar directory extraction issues."""

    @pytest.fixture
    def temp_dir(self):
        """Create a temporary directory for test output."""
        with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir:
            yield tmpdir

    @pytest.fixture
    def temp_tar_path(self, temp_dir):
        """Create a temporary tar file path."""
        return os.path.join(temp_dir, "test_collection.tar.gz")

    def create_tar_with_directory_variations(self, tar_path, include_trailing_sep=False):
        """
        Create a tar archive with directory entries.

        This function creates a tar archive that may have directory entries
        stored with or without trailing separators, which is the core of the
        fragility issue.

        :param tar_path: Path to create the tar file
        :param include_trailing_sep: If True, create directory entries WITH trailing separators
        """
        with tarfile.open(tar_path, mode='w:gz') as tar:
            # Create MANIFEST.json
            manifest = {
                'collection_info': {
                    'namespace': 'test',
                    'name': 'collection',
                    'version': '1.0.0',
                    'authors': ['Test'],
                    'license': ['GPL-3.0-or-later'],
                    'description': 'Test collection',
                    'repository': None,
                    'documentation': None,
                    'homepage': None,
                    'issues': None,
                    'readme': None,
                    'tags': [],
                    'dependencies': {},
                }
            }
            manifest_bytes = json.dumps(manifest).encode('utf-8')
            manifest_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name='MANIFEST.json')
            manifest_info.size = len(manifest_bytes)
            tar.addfile(manifest_info, io.BytesIO(manifest_bytes))

            # Create FILES.json with both file and directory entries
            files_manifest = {
                'files': [
                    {'name': '.', 'ftype': 'dir', 'chksum_type': None, 'chksum_sha256': None, 'format': 1},
                    # Directories - the key test is how these are named
                    {'name': 'plugins', 'ftype': 'dir', 'chksum_type': None, 'chksum_sha256': None, 'format': 1},
                    {'name': 'plugins/modules', 'ftype': 'dir', 'chksum_type': None, 'chksum_sha256': None, 'format': 1},
                    # Files
                    {'name': 'plugins/modules/test.py', 'ftype': 'file', 'chksum_type': 'sha256', 'chksum_sha256': 'abc123', 'format': 1},
                    {'name': 'README.md', 'ftype': 'file', 'chksum_type': 'sha256', 'chksum_sha256': 'def456', 'format': 1},
                ]
            }
            files_bytes = json.dumps(files_manifest).encode('utf-8')
            files_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name='FILES.json')
            files_info.size = len(files_bytes)
            tar.addfile(files_info, io.BytesIO(files_bytes))

            # Add directories to tar with or without trailing separators
            # This is the fragility: different tar creators may add trailing separators
            if include_trailing_sep:
                # Standard tar format: directories have trailing separators
                dir_entries = ['plugins/', 'plugins/modules/']
            else:
                # Non-standard but possible: directories without trailing separators
                dir_entries = ['plugins', 'plugins/modules']

            for dir_name in dir_entries:
                dir_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name=dir_name)
                dir_info.type = tarfile.DIRTYPE
                dir_info.mode = 0o755
                tar.addfile(dir_info)

            # Add a simple file
            file_content = b"print('test')"
            file_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name='plugins/modules/test.py')
            file_info.size = len(file_content)
            tar.addfile(file_info, io.BytesIO(file_content))

            # Add README
            readme_content = b"# Test Collection"
            readme_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name='README.md')
            readme_info.size = len(readme_content)
            tar.addfile(readme_info, io.BytesIO(readme_content))

    def test_tar_extraction_with_trailing_separator_directories(self, temp_tar_path, temp_dir):
        """
        Test extraction when tar has directories with trailing separators.

        This tests the standard tar format where directories are stored with
        trailing separators. The normalized cache should handle this correctly.
        """
        self.create_tar_with_directory_variations(temp_tar_path, include_trailing_sep=True)

        # Open the tar and manually test the cache
        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='r:gz') as tar:
            # Build the normalized cache like the code does
            tar._ansible_normalized_cache = {
                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in tar.getmembers()
            }

            # Try to extract directories as the code does in FILES.json processing
            collection_path = os.path.join(temp_dir, 'extract_test_1')
            os.makedirs(collection_path)

            # Extract 'plugins' directory (no trailing separator in manifest)
            # But tar has it as 'plugins/' (with trailing separator)
            try:
                _extract_tar_dir(tar, 'plugins', collection_path.encode('utf-8'))
                assert os.path.isdir(os.path.join(collection_path, 'plugins'))
            except AnsibleError as e:
                pytest.fail(f"Failed to extract directory with trailing separator: {e}")

    def test_tar_extraction_without_trailing_separator_directories(self, temp_tar_path, temp_dir):
        """
        Test extraction when tar has directories WITHOUT trailing separators.

        This tests a non-standard but possible tar layout. This is where the
        fragility becomes apparent - the custom cache may fail or behave
        unexpectedly with this format.
        """
        self.create_tar_with_directory_variations(temp_tar_path, include_trailing_sep=False)

        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='r:gz') as tar:
            # Check what members are actually in the tar
            members = {m.name for m in tar.getmembers()}
            print(f"Tar members: {members}")

            # Build the normalized cache like the code does
            tar._ansible_normalized_cache = {
                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in tar.getmembers()
            }

            print(f"Normalized cache keys: {tar._ansible_normalized_cache.keys()}")

            # Try to extract directories
            collection_path = os.path.join(temp_dir, 'extract_test_2')
            os.makedirs(collection_path)

            # Extract 'plugins' directory
            # If tar has 'plugins' (no separator), cache should have 'plugins'
            try:
                _extract_tar_dir(tar, 'plugins', collection_path.encode('utf-8'))
                assert os.path.isdir(os.path.join(collection_path, 'plugins'))
            except AnsibleError as e:
                pytest.fail(f"Failed to extract directory without trailing separator: {e}")

    def test_normalized_cache_key_collision(self, temp_tar_path):
        """
        Test that demonstrates the fragility: cache key collision.

        When a tar archive could theoretically have both 'plugins' and 'plugins/'
        as separate entries, the normalized cache would create a collision where
        the last one overwrites the first. This shows why the custom cache approach
        is fragile and why the standard tarfile.getmember() API is better.
        """
        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='w:gz') as tar:
            # Create a directory entry WITHOUT trailing separator
            dir_info1 = tarfile.TarInfo(name='plugins')
            dir_info1.type = tarfile.DIRTYPE
            dir_info1.mode = 0o755
            tar.addfile(dir_info1)

            # Create a directory entry WITH trailing separator
            # In a real scenario, these could be added by different tools
            # or in different orders
            dir_info2 = tarfile.TarInfo(name='plugins/')
            dir_info2.type = tarfile.DIRTYPE
            dir_info2.mode = 0o755
            tar.addfile(dir_info2)

        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='r:gz') as tar:
            members = {m.name for m in tar.getmembers()}

            # Verify both entries exist in the tar
            assert 'plugins' in members, "Tar should have 'plugins' entry"
            assert 'plugins/' in members, "Tar should have 'plugins/' entry"

            # Now build the normalized cache - this is where the fragility occurs
            cache = {
                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in tar.getmembers()
            }

            # The cache will only have ONE entry 'plugins' since both normalized to the same key
            assert len(cache) == 1, "Cache should have only 1 entry after normalization"
            assert 'plugins' in cache, "Cache should have 'plugins' key"

            # This is the fragility: we lose information about which tar member
            # was used because the normalization collapses them
            # The actual tar has 2 members, but the cache only tracks 1
            # If code logic depended on having both entries, it would fail
            print(f"Tar has {len(members)} members but normalized cache has {len(cache)} entries")

    def test_custom_cache_vs_standard_getmember(self, temp_tar_path, temp_dir):
        """
        Test that shows why the custom cache is fragile compared to getmember().

        This test demonstrates that the standard tarfile.getmember() API correctly
        handles directory lookups with or without trailing separators, making the
        custom cache workaround unnecessary on Python 3.11+.
        """
        self.create_tar_with_directory_variations(temp_tar_path, include_trailing_sep=True)

        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='r:gz') as tar:
            # Method 1: Using the fragile custom cache
            custom_cache = {
                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in tar.getmembers()
            }

            # Method 2: Using standard getmember() API
            # Modern Python versions (3.11+) handle both cases correctly

            # Test 1: Look up 'plugins' (no trailing separator in tar but with separator in manifest)
            try:
                # With custom cache
                cached_member = custom_cache.get('plugins')

                # With standard API - on modern Python, this should work
                # even if the tar has 'plugins/' with a trailing separator
                try:
                    standard_member = tar.getmember('plugins')
                    print(f"Standard getmember('plugins') found: {standard_member.name}")
                except KeyError:
                    # On older Python, this might fail, requiring the workaround
                    print("Standard getmember('plugins') failed - this is why the workaround was needed")
                    standard_member = None

                # The fragility is that if getmember() behavior changes between
                # Python versions, the custom cache becomes a band-aid that
                # masks the real issue
                assert cached_member is not None, "Custom cache should find directory"
            except Exception as e:
                pytest.fail(f"Directory lookup failed: {e}")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    pytest.main([__file__, '-v'])

def test_fragility_with_trailing_sep_mismatch(self, temp_tar_path, temp_dir):
        """
        Test that demonstrates the fragility of the custom cache with standard tar format.

        In standard tar format, directories are stored with trailing separators.
        The custom cache strips these, relying on the normalization working correctly.
        This test shows that if the tar member format varies (some with, some without
        trailing separators), the custom cache approach becomes fragile.
        """
        # Create a tar with directories having trailing separators (standard format)
        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='w:gz') as tar:
            # Create manifest
            manifest = {
                'collection_info': {
                    'namespace': 'test',
                    'name': 'collection',
                    'version': '1.0.0',
                    'authors': ['Test'],
                    'license': ['GPL-3.0-or-later'],
                    'description': 'Test collection',
                    'repository': None,
                    'documentation': None,
                    'homepage': None,
                    'issues': None,
                    'readme': None,
                    'tags': [],
                    'dependencies': {},
                }
            }
            manifest_bytes = json.dumps(manifest).encode('utf-8')
            manifest_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name='MANIFEST.json')
            manifest_info.size = len(manifest_bytes)
            tar.addfile(manifest_info, io.BytesIO(manifest_bytes))

            # Create FILES.json
            files_manifest = {
                'files': [
                    {'name': '.', 'ftype': 'dir', 'chksum_type': None, 'chksum_sha256': None, 'format': 1},
                    {'name': 'plugins', 'ftype': 'dir', 'chksum_type': None, 'chksum_sha256': None, 'format': 1},
                ]
            }
            files_bytes = json.dumps(files_manifest).encode('utf-8')
            files_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name='FILES.json')
            files_info.size = len(files_bytes)
            tar.addfile(files_info, io.BytesIO(files_bytes))

            # Add directory with trailing separator (standard tar format)
            dir_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name='plugins/')
            dir_info.type = tarfile.DIRTYPE
            dir_info.mode = 0o755
            tar.addfile(dir_info)

        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='r:gz') as tar:
            # Verify the tar has 'plugins/' with trailing separator
            members = {m.name for m in tar.getmembers()}
            assert 'plugins/' in members, "Tar should have 'plugins/' with trailing separator"

            # Build the custom cache like the code does
            tar._ansible_normalized_cache = {
                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in tar.getmembers()
            }

            # The cache converts 'plugins/' → 'plugins'
            assert 'plugins' in tar._ansible_normalized_cache
            cached_member = tar._ansible_normalized_cache['plugins']
            assert cached_member.name == 'plugins/', "Cache should point to 'plugins/' member"

            # Now test extraction - FILES.json says to extract 'plugins' (no separator)
            # The custom cache normalizes this and finds 'plugins/'
            collection_path = os.path.join(temp_dir, 'extract_test')
            os.makedirs(collection_path)

            try:
                _extract_tar_dir(tar, 'plugins', collection_path.encode('utf-8'))
                extracted_path = os.path.join(collection_path, 'plugins')
                assert os.path.isdir(extracted_path), "Directory should be extracted"
                print(f"Successfully extracted directory despite tar/manifest mismatch")
            except AnsibleError as e:
                pytest.fail(f"Should be able to extract directory: {e}")

    def test_custom_cache_is_workaround_not_solution(self, temp_tar_path):
        """
        Test that demonstrates the custom cache is a Python-version-specific workaround.

        The custom cache was added as a workaround for CPython bug 47231, which affected
        how tarfile.getmember() handles directory name lookups. On Python 3.11+, this
        bug is fixed, making the workaround unnecessary and just adding fragility.

        This test shows that the workaround works, but only because it bypasses the
        tarfile API rather than fixing the real issue.
        """
        self.create_tar_with_directory_variations(temp_tar_path, include_trailing_sep=True)

        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='r:gz') as tar:
            # The custom cache approach
            custom_cache = {
                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in tar.getmembers()
            }

            # On Python 3.11+, getmember() should work fine for directory lookups
            # But the custom cache was added for older Python versions where it didn't
            try:
                # Try to use standard getmember() API
                member_via_api = tar.getmember('plugins')
                print(f"Standard API found 'plugins': {member_via_api.name}")

                # The custom cache achieves the same result
                member_via_cache = custom_cache.get('plugins')
                assert member_via_cache is not None
                print(f"Custom cache found 'plugins': {member_via_cache.name}")

                # Both work on Python 3.11+, but the custom cache adds complexity
                # and fragility that isn't needed
                print("Both approaches work on Python 3.11+, but custom cache adds unnecessary complexity")
            except KeyError:
                print("Standard API fails - custom cache workaround would be needed on older Python")

        """

        In standard tar format, directories are stored with trailing separators.
        The custom cache strips these, relying on the normalization working correctly.
        This test shows that if the tar member format varies (some with, some without
        trailing separators), the custom cache approach becomes fragile.
        """

            manifest = {

            }

            files_info = tarfile.TarInfo(name='FILES.json')

    def test_custom_cache_is_workaround_not_solution(self, temp_tar_path):
        """
        Test that demonstrates the custom cache is a Python-version-specific workaround.

        The custom cache was added as a workaround for CPython bug 47231, which affected
        how tarfile.getmember() handles directory name lookups. On Python 3.11+, this
        bug is fixed, making the workaround unnecessary and just adding fragility.

        This test shows that the workaround works, but only because it bypasses the
        tarfile API rather than fixing the real issue.
        """
        self.create_tar_with_directory_variations(temp_tar_path, include_trailing_sep=True)

        with tarfile.open(temp_tar_path, mode='r:gz') as tar:
            # The custom cache approach
            custom_cache = {
                m.name.removesuffix(os.path.sep): m for m in tar.getmembers()
            }

            # On Python 3.11+, getmember() should work fine for directory lookups
            # But the custom cache was added for older Python versions where it didn't
            try:
                # Try to use standard getmember() API
                member_via_api = tar.getmember('plugins')
                print(f"Standard API found 'plugins': {member_via_api.name}")

                # The custom cache achieves the same result
                member_via_cache = custom_cache.get('plugins')
                assert member_via_cache is not None
                print(f"Custom cache found 'plugins': {member_via_cache.name}")

                # Both work on Python 3.11+, but the custom cache adds complexity
                # and fragility that isn't needed
                print("Both approaches work on Python 3.11+, but custom cache adds unnecessary complexity")
            except KeyError:
                print("Standard API fails - custom cache workaround would be needed on older Python")
